speaker: interviewer
text: August 22, 1995. This is tape four. Mr. Zelikson, you were telling us about your experience working in the butcher shop in Brooklyn, I believe.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But before you get to that, I just want to clarify one thing. The amount of time you were at Bergen-Belsen. And before you had said you came from Bergen-Belsen to this country at about 19. But was that accurate?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1949, January 26.

speaker: interviewer
text: You came to this country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how old were then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 17 1/2.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. OK. So altogether, you had been in Bergen-Belsen for how long at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say about at least six months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. OK. Anyway, back to the story of the butcher shop.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he wasn't paying you quite enough.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was working over there from 6 o'clock in the morning to about around 8 o'clock at night. And after 8 o'clock I had to clean the machines. I was plucking the chicken. I had to clean all that. And he was paying me $25 an hour.

speaker: interviewer
text: An hour?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am sorry. $25 a week. And I-- of course, I couldn't make any money. I told him I want a raise. He says he's going to give me $5. I says, no, I want $10. He says he can't afford it. He won't give me.

speaker: interviewer
text: I says, then forget it. I'm not going to work for you. And give me the $25. He says he hasn't got it. I says to him, you just took in over $500. How can you not have $25? He says he-- he pays by check with the accountant. I should come in Monday. That was Friday. I says to him, no, if I don't collect, I'll send somebody to collect. And he got mad at me. He says, you scaring me? I said, I'm not scaring you. I'm telling the truth. He took a-- the sharpener, what you sharpen the knife, and he went after me. I was strong. He was an old man. He was in the 70. I says, Mr. Marcus, put away the iron before I break your arm. He says, you going to do that? I says, if you take the iron I'll break your arm. So he put away the iron. He took the big butcher knife and went after me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I got mad, and I picked him up with one hand to put him on the hook. But lucky for him he-- he was in the freezer, he had a [INAUDIBLE] on. A heavy [INAUDIBLE]. So the hook didn't penetrate him. And his wife start to scream, he's killing my husband, and he's a greener, and people called the police.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one of the policeman was a [NON-ENGLISH], and he spoke fluent Yiddish-- Jewish. Because I couldn't speak a word English. And he says, what happened? And I explain to him. He says, put your hand away behind your back. And I heard he clicked in handcuffs me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I says to him, listen. Do me a favor. Send me back to Europe. I don't want to be in United States because if they do the same thing what the Nazis did, I don't want to be in this country. I-- I'll go back to Europe. He says-- not so fast, he says. Listen, I'm going to call up-- I have a friend of mine who's a judge, Judge Leibovitz. I'll call him up and I'll bring you there, and you tell him the exactly what you told me.

speaker: interviewer
text: I came to Judge Leibovitz. And his wife was a very fine woman. And Judge Leibovitz spoke fluent Jewish. And he told me, tell what happened. What did you do? Why did you hang him on the hook?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the wife says to him, listen, first he-- the man didn't eat nothing. Let's eat supper. Then-- and she asked me if I'm hungry. I told her, yeah. I didn't eat. And she gave me supper. And we sat down and we-- I told them the whole story, that I used to pluck the chicken. And he took away the money. And I worked from 6 o'clock to 8 o'clock at night. And he told his [NON-ENGLISH] to bring the butcher right away over to him, and to bring the checkbook. He brought the-- took about an hour or so, and he brought him over. And he came with the checkbook. And he says, you owe this man $1,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this in the judge's house?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the judge house. It was on Pitkin Avenue. You owe the man $1,000. You supposed to pay him $0.75 an hour, which you never did. According to my figure, you paid him $0.10 an hour. You supposed to pay him at time and a half till 6 o'clock. After 6 o'clock, you're supposed to pay him double time. And you didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now I'm going to fine you $5,000 for exploit labor. And you're lucky that I don't send you to jail. And he start to scream, I haven't got money. Where I'm going to get the money? So the judge says, according your checkbook, you have enough for 10 times. He says, if you open your mouth once more, I'll double it. So I came out with $1,000. The judge told me if I ever need a job to call him up and he'll get me a job. And then what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: In meantime, a friend of mine, he met me. And he says, Shaska, what are you doing? You're working? And I told him-- I told him the story. He says, I have another friend that is looking for a glazier. I said, a glazier, then. A glazier.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he took me-- he took to-- his name was [INAUDIBLE]. And he took me in, and he showed me how to cut glass. He said-- he asked me, are you afraid getting cut? I says, no.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as soon as he open his mouth, I cut my-- right here, my thumb. But-- I knew that was a big cut. And I took a rag and covered it. He says, no, no, no. I'll take you to the hospital. I says, no. Let me-- let me finish my job, and then you take me to the hospital. He says, no. I'm taking you to the hospital right now. Took to hospital. They-- put about nine stitches. He says, now I know you're not afraid of glass. I worked for him about two years. And in two years he give me a-- he used to pay me $40, but in three installments. He didn't have no payroll. He was a gambler. He used to go-- used to leave the wife in the store and go to the track.

speaker: interviewer
text: After two years, I says, I can't do nothing. I'm not going nowhere. I want to get married. I want to have a family. I says, I want to become your partner. He say, my partner? You have money?

speaker: interviewee
text: I says, I got $5,000 reparation from Germany for being in the concentration camp. That's all I have. I haven't got nothing. So $5,000 is a lot of money. I'm going back about 44 years ago. That was a lot of money.

speaker: interviewer
text: He says, good. Give me-- bring me the money. I says, no. That's not the way it works. He says, what do you mean? I says, first we going to have a payroll. Second, we going to hire one man. And we buy a truck. You haven't got a truck.

speaker: interviewee
text: You can't work. Because he used to-- a lot of people call him to put in glass in the windows. He used to call up a bigger glazier and tell him to figure in-- me in. I told him, I don't want that. I'm going to do my own installation, and I'm going to do with a man-- do it. And I don't have to give it to the big companies.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he start-- he got mad at me. He says, you greenhorn, you tell me what to do? I says, as long you take my money, I'll tell you exactly what to do. And no more going to the race track. After work, you can do what you want. But during the work, you stay inside. I'll stay outside. And I'll do all the work-- you do inside the work.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, but you give me the $5,000. Says that's the agreement. And we going to sign. We call up a lawyer and sign the agreement. Otherwise I'm not going to give you the $5,000. He says, but that belong-- the $5,000 belongs to me. You give me just for goodwill. I says, no. That's the way I want it. You like it, OK. You don't like it, I'm not going to give you $5,000. I'll go someplace else. He--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you become partners?

speaker: interviewee
text: He took the $5,000, became partners. And we made a payroll. We got a truck. And the next time we got another truck. And I worked for him about-- oh, what, about 8 years. And the kid was not-- not my partner. He worked to clean the-- after school, he cleaned the-- the store.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this? The person who worked for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Used-- yeah. He worked for me. He was 12 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you married at this time yet?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not yet. So he-- he-- he worked for me. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I made a little bit money. And I says, it's time to get married. So I started to look. And of course, I couldn't speak that good English. I was-- yeah. I was working from 7 o'clock till 7:30 at night in the store. And from 8 o'clock at night till 12 o'clock I was going in school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: And now I'm a reader. I was about 21.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were you studying in school? I'm sorry. I didn't hear what you said.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went in high school at night. Of course, I studied the English. I studied arithmetic, English history, and other curriculars that was necessary to survive. And then I was looking for a girl to go out. I met one. I met another where I didn't like it. And my wife's-- my wife's girlfriend, she knew very well her sister. And her sister called me up. And she says, I have somebody you could meet. And when I came and I saw her and I liked her, we went out. We went out-- I took her to Coney Island. And she says she never wins anything, to throw the rings over the bottle. And I give her money. She put it. On the first toss she got the ring over the bottle, and she got a beautiful poodle. And she loved-- she fell in love with it. And, of course, we fell in love. And I told her-- because I suffer from headaches, sometimes seizures, because when the German broke my head. And I said, listen. I have to tell the truth what's wrong with me. If you want it, and you understand, then we're going to get engaged. And if you don't, I walk. And I told her what happened to me. As a matter of fact, I stood her up. I went away to Canada for a weekend. I supposed to go with her. Because I was afraid she wouldn't take me. And we got together pretty good, and we got engaged and we got married. And-- no. Before the marriage, that-- I have to correct it. A week before married, I was working on the second floor and sitting in the-- putting in a glass in the window. And I fell out from the window with the frame--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh my.

speaker: interviewee
text: --in my hand. Luckily, there was a mattress on the sidewalk, and I fell on the mattress, and I broke my left leg. She came to the hospital. And I says, now we have to postpone the wedding. She says, over my dead body.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at that time, the rabbi came to visit me in the hospital. And she says, Rabbi, can he postponed it? He says, oh, no. Even if he has to get married in a hospital, you get married in the hospital. In Jewish religion, you don't postpone a wedding. I got married. In the hospital? Or out?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, out of the hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good.

speaker: interviewee
text: In a synagogue, I had a cast about up to-- up to right here, my tie. It was about 35 pounds, the cast. And I took my wife to Niagara Falls with the cast. [LAUGHS] Don't ask how I managed. And we managed. And then, of course, we had our first daughter. And then we had my son. And so on. We had five children. We lived very happy. My wife was happy. I was happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in meantime, my guy that used to work for me, the was was the Vietnam War. He had to go to the war. When he came back to the war, he says, Sam-- when the war was finished-- do I still have a job? I says, sure, yeah. And my older partner, [INAUDIBLE], he says he wants to retire. He wants to go to Florida. I says, if you want to become my partner, now is your chance. I says, how much he wants for the-- his part of the business. He says, $10,000. I says, $10,000 is a steal. I have three trucks. I have $25,000 in stock. I have machines. I say, what's $10,000? He says, but Sam, I haven't got it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Incidentally, I'm Jewish and he was Spanish. But I knew the family, I knew him when he was in his diaper. And very fine-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't have to worry about as far as honesty. Very honest.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I says to him, what can-- how much can you get from the family? He says, $2,500. So I lend him the $7,500. And he became partner. We were partner for 21 years. Not a lot an argument. Not even a lot word to each other. What was you marriage date? You said you-- when you got married with your broken leg?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1956, I got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were partners with this man for 5-- for 25 years. Is that what you said?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now you're retired?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Now retired. But then I went and I did another job. And as a matter of fact, in Riverdale's-- in Jewish Center. I went up 18 feet in the air to put in a glass. And I says to my man, stay by the ladder and watch the ladder. And I was up on top putting in the glass. And my man-- a girl passed by with a short skirt, and he let go of the ladder to look for the girl. And I came down, and I broke both legs. I broke both heels and both ankles. And I was in the hospital for about 11 months. And one specialist says, forget-- forget about walking. You won't walk no more. And my-- another specialist says, hey, maybe in 10, 15 years. And the third one says, it's up to you. If you want to walk and if you can do the pain--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- oh, about 31. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah. About 31, I was. If you can endure the pain, you could do it. And I almost fell again. I went up to do the exercise for therapy, and everything started to circle. And I fell. And she wanted to take me back. I says, no. I'm going to try it again. And I tried it and fell again. And I went up again until I made it. I started little by little to walk. It took me 15 months. I start to walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you continued being a glazier?

speaker: interviewee
text: I continued to be a glazier. Then we hire-- we hired another guy, and that was my partner's brother. It was on 14th Street. At that-- at that time, it was what-- now it's maybe 15 years ago. And I said to him we had to put in a big window in a store. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When we lift up the glass-- slow, because you can't hit the iron. And he was a 350 pounder. I was on one side. He was on the other side. And he lift up the glass, and he strung the iron, and the glass came down and cut my arm right off. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Two women in the store, they passed out. My worker went on the floor, passed out. I had to call an ambulance. I had-- I took a-- a rope of a-- rope. I tied it around my arm to stop the bleeding. And there was a-- a basket. I ran in the freezer. They had a refrigerator and ice. I put my hand in the basket while I was bleeding.

speaker: interviewer
text: The ambulance came. And they're laying on the floor. I'm sitting on the chair with a puddle of blood. And he says, who is the patient? I says, you must be kidding. I says, I'm bleeding. They're laying on floor. They passed out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, he put the bandage around, took to-- took me to Bellevue Hospital. And there was a Dr. Shaw, the same doctor that operated on the Renee Katz girl, that--

speaker: interviewer
text: The train.

speaker: interviewee
text: --cut-- right. Onto the subway. Cut her arm. He told me-- he says, the operation is going to take between three to four hours. And he says to me, do you want to see it, or you want a needle to knock you out? I says, whatever you want to do, I want to see it. So he give me a local onto the arm. And the local was for four hours. But the operation took 10 and 1/2 hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your hand wasn't completely off, was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was off.

speaker: interviewer
text: Completely separate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was off. I had 67 stitches outside, and about 120 inside. With a laser, they did it. And then they had to put plastic surgery to do it the right way. And he told me he's very sorry that the operation is going to take that long. And he couldn't give me one anesthetic on top another one, because it weakens your heart. He says, you have to bear with me. So for 6 and 1/2 hours, don't ask what I went through. And then I went for my work-- I went from lunch, to go back to the store. A woman-- it was raining-- and a woman was in a rush. And the light was in my favor. I was walking. And she was driving a car. She had to pick up her child. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A dark woman. And she speeded up, and she hit me and threw me up 20 feet in the air.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this? How old were you now? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- that was in 1991.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1991. And the police was right by the corner. And one-- that what my partner told me. Because everybody came running, they heard a crash. I was unconscious. And one policeman says-- he took my vital. He says, forget it. Call the morgue. He's finished. And the-- the other policeman says, let me take it, another look. And he says, no. He's still breathing. And they took me to Jacobi Hospital. In Jacobi Hospital, I was there two weeks. They took six x-rays, and they never discovered that I had two broken ribs.

speaker: interviewer
text: I had two pinched nerves on my neck. I still-- I could move this way, but I can't move the other way because of the pinched nerves. And a-- a patient next to me says, you know what today is? I says, what? He says, today's $90 million lottery. That was on Saturday. I says, lotto? $90 million? I'm going to play. But I'll wait for my daughter, because she's supposed to come over-- my daughter Alice-- to take me home. So when my daughter came, I says, Alice. Before we go home, let's play the lotto. I says, it's $90 million.

speaker: interviewee
text: She says, Dad. First I'll take you home, and then we'll talk about it. She took me home. I says, forget it. Let's pick some numbers. She say, but Dad, I haven't got the money. Don't ask me why, I took out $16. I give her $16. And she, with my other daughter, Linda, they went downstairs and they bought the tickets. And we were watching, 8 o'clock at night. One number comes up. The second number come up. The third, the fourth. I says to my wife, hey, we will have some money because four numbers, you get money. And then the fifth number. I says, oh. Now another. So good. And then the sixth number came up. The kids got-- start to scream. And I say, wait. We are not going to do anything. I'll call up the lotto and find out if it's true.

speaker: interviewer
text: I call up the lotto, and they say I called in your numbers. It's confirmed. You just won $90 million. And I says to my wife, what the heck I'm going to do with $90 million? I never had that kind of money. Impossible.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the next day on Sunday, there was eight more people. And they picked us up with a limo, took us downtown, and took pictures. And all the newspapers-- oh, no. Before they took us down-- yeah. After they took us downtown, all the newspaper people came in my house and took pictures. And I told them I don't want no telephone number or no street number. If they want it, they could do it. So they agreed. And they-- I was on channel 5, and channel 7, and channel 11, 4, 2-- you name it.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was exciting.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And then they ask me what I'm going to do with my-- our wealth. I says, I'm going to help Israel. I says, I survived to help Israel, and I want to help Israel. And I put quite a few money-- I bought Israel bunch to give it to Israel. And when I went to Israel, I was a big guest. I was.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's beautiful. It's a nice story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, so-- and then we went to Hawaii, and we went other places. And I sold the house in Yonkers, and I bought this house.

speaker: interviewer
text: A beautiful house in Mount Kisco.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right--

speaker: interviewee
text: I am very happy, and now my daughter expect twins. So I'll-- I'll have grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: Beautiful. I'm going-- I'm going to thank you very much, Mr. Zelikson.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're very welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: And soon, your family will come in.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Thank you. I'll tell them.
